title: How to Thaw a Frozen Pipe (Safely) — Step-by-Step
question: No water is coming out of a faucet on a freezing day and I think a pipe is frozen. How do I thaw it without bursting it, and how do I know if it already burst?
summary: If a faucet barely trickles in a cold snap, a pipe is likely frozen. Open the faucet, find the frozen section (usually along an exterior wall, in a crawlspace, or the garage), and warm it gradually with a hair dryer or heat lamp while the tap stays open. Never use an open flame. The real danger is a pipe that already cracked while frozen — it leaks when it thaws — so know where your main shutoff is before you start.

commonCauses.cause: Water frozen in a pipe along an exterior wall or in an unheated space (crawlspace, garage, attic, under a sink on an outside wall). Ice blocks flow and expands, stressing the pipe.
commonCauses.quickCheck: One faucet trickles or gives nothing while others work, on a sub-freezing day. Trace which pipe feeds that faucet — the frozen spot is usually on an exterior wall or in an unheated run.

commonCauses.cause: Already-cracked pipe from the freeze. Pipes burst from the expanding ice but may not leak until they thaw and water flows again.
commonCauses.quickCheck: Inspect the frozen run for bulges, splits, or frost on a joint. If you find damage, do NOT thaw until the main water is shut off.

commonCauses.cause: Outdoor hose bib or sprinkler line left connected/charged over winter.
commonCauses.quickCheck: Check whether a hose was left attached or the line wasn't drained/shut at the indoor valve before the freeze.

fixes: Locate your main water shutoff first, before thawing — if the pipe cracked, you'll need to stop the water fast when it thaws.
Open the affected faucet (both hot and cold). Running water and the open tap relieve pressure and help melt the ice.
Find the frozen section — feel for the coldest spot, look for frost, usually on an exterior wall or unheated space.
Warm it gradually from the faucet end toward the frozen area with a hair dryer, heat lamp, or towels soaked in hot water. Move the heat along the pipe; don't park it on one spot.
NEVER use a blowtorch, propane heater, or any open flame — it can burst the pipe, damage it, or start a fire.
Keep going until full pressure returns. Then check the whole run for leaks now that it's flowing.
If you can't reach the frozen section (inside a wall), or the pipe is cracked, shut off the main and call a plumber.
Prevent a repeat: insulate exposed pipes, seal drafts near them, and let a faucet drip during hard freezes.
diyVsPro: DIY for accessible pipes you can reach and warm gradually. Call a plumber immediately if you find a crack, bulge, or leak; if the frozen pipe is inside a finished wall or ceiling you can't access; if water doesn't return after reasonable thawing; or if you're not confident locating your shutoff. A burst pipe can flood and cause major water damage fast, so when in doubt, shut the main and call.
typicalCostUSD: $0-$30 DIY (you likely own a hair dryer; pipe insulation is cheap). Emergency plumber for a burst or inaccessible pipe runs $150-$500+, far more if there's water damage.
timeEstimate: 30-60 minutes to thaw an accessible pipe; longer for hard freezes.
safetyNote: Never thaw a pipe with an open flame — it's the leading cause of pipe-thawing fires and bursts. Keep electric heat sources away from standing water (shock risk) and use GFCI protection. Know where your main shutoff is before you start, because a pipe that froze may already be cracked and will leak the moment it thaws. If you see a crack or can't reach the pipe, shut the main and call a pro.
toolsParts: Hair dryer, heat lamp, or space heater (no open flame)
Knowledge/location of the main water shutoff
